name: Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum
epithet: The One for Whom Heaven Spoke
title: Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum (RA): The One for Whom Heaven Spoke
meta_description: The story of Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum (RA), the blind companion for whom the Qur'an was revealed twice. The man behind Surah Abasa, a muezzin of Madinah, and a flag-bearer at Qadisiyyah.
keywords: Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum, story of Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum, Surah Abasa, blind companion, muezzin of the Prophet, sahaba stories, disability in Islam
lede: Most people know him through a single moment: a man hurrying toward the Prophet ﷺ, a slight frown, and a verse of the Qur'an that followed. But that moment was never meant to shame him. It was sent to honour him, and to change the way an entire community would look at people forever.

This is the story of Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum (may Allah be pleased with him): a man born without sight, of noble Makkan blood, for whom the Qur'an was revealed not once but twice, and who would one day die on a battlefield holding the banner he could not see.

sections.h2: The man behind one famous moment
paras: He came from good standing in Makkah. Both sides of his family belonged to Quraysh, and he was a first cousin of the Prophet's wife Khadijah (RA): her aunt was his mother. So when people picture him as one of the poor and the powerless, they only have half the picture. He carried a respected lineage. What set him apart was something else entirely.

Abdullah was the only companion we know of who was born blind. Others lost their sight in old age, but he never saw the world at all. His mother became known as Umm Maktum, from a word that means concealed, because her son's sight was hidden from him. People called her that out of sympathy for the child who could not see.

Because of his standing, he was spared the open torture that fell on companions like Bilal. We know of no persecution against him. He had heard of the Prophet's character long before the call began, and as soon as he heard the message, he believed. He was among the very earliest Muslims, accepting Islam before the first believers even gathered in the house of al-Arqam.

The Prophet ﷺ was sitting with the leaders of Quraysh: Utbah, his brother Shaybah, Umayyah ibn Khalaf, Abu Jahl, and al-Walid. These were powerful men who had mocked him and persecuted those around him, and now they seemed willing, for once, to listen. He hoped that if such men accepted the message, others would follow, and the early Muslims might finally find some relief.

Into that gathering came Abdullah, rushing, calling out, "O Muhammad, teach me from what Allah has taught you." He could not see who else was there. This was simply how he and the Prophet ﷺ were with one another: the Prophet had made himself so available that Abdullah would come, find him, place a hand on him, and be answered for as long as he wished.

The Prophet ﷺ did not push him away or tell him to leave. He simply drew his brows together, a flicker of being interrupted, and turned back to the men he was hoping to reach. Abdullah never saw it. But the leaders saw it. And then the revelation came.

ayah.text: He frowned and turned away when the blind man came to him: for all you know, he might have grown in spirit, or taken note of something useful to him.

sections.h2: Sixteen verses, and a changed heart
paras: Allah revealed sixteen verses for this one moment. Not a private word to the Prophet ﷺ, but Qur'an that would be recited until the end of time. The same revelation that gently corrected him also raised Abdullah, asking why the man full of eagerness and awe should be passed over for those who felt no need of guidance at all.

This was never humiliation for the Prophet ﷺ. It was an honour, a sign of how carefully Allah was teaching him, and through him, teaching everyone who came after. The lesson was a shift in vision: that the worth of a person is not measured by status or strength, but by the heart turning toward God.

From then on, whenever the Prophet ﷺ saw Abdullah, he would stand to greet him and say, "Welcome to the one on whose behalf my Lord admonished me." He did not hold the moment against him. He treated him as someone special, because Allah had spoken from above the seven heavens on his behalf.

There is a quiet footnote to the story. Every one of those proud men in the gathering, except al-Walid who died earlier, was later killed at the Battle of Badr. The world had honoured them and overlooked the blind man. Allah honoured the one the world had overlooked.

When the door to Madinah opened, Abdullah was among the very first to walk through it. He was the second person to make the migration, after Mus'ab ibn Umayr. The two of them were sent ahead of the Prophet ﷺ for one purpose: to teach the people the Qur'an.

So while the Muslims of Madinah waited for the Prophet ﷺ to arrive, it was these two men, going from house to house, who taught them the verses being revealed in distant Makkah. A blind man and a young noble, carrying the words of God into homes that had never heard them.

Then came Badr, and with it his first taste of being left behind. He longed to go out with the Prophet ﷺ, but he was not placed among the fighters, and it grieved him deeply. He was one of the earliest Muslims, the Qur'an had already been revealed on his behalf, and yet he could not stand in the line.

It would be easy to read his sadness as wounded pride. It was the opposite. A man who feels no closeness to God is relieved to be excused. A man whose heart is full of Him aches to be counted in. Abdullah ached to be counted in.

He did not look for ways out of striving. He looked for ways in. When the heart is truly in awe of Allah, it does not search for permission to do less. It searches for any way to do more. So Abdullah made a simple, aching dua: "O Allah, reveal my excuse." He wanted to hear his own situation answered in the Qur'an.

One day the verse came that those who sit at home are not equal to those who strive in the path of Allah. Abdullah heard it and his heart sank further. He said, "O Messenger of Allah, what about me? I cannot see." Revelation came over the Prophet ﷺ again, and when it lifted, a few words had been added to the verse:

ayah.text: Those believers who stay at home, apart from those with an incapacity, are not equal to those who commit themselves and their possessions to striving in God's way.

paras: In Madinah, Abdullah was given honours that overturned every assumption of the time. The Prophet ﷺ made him a caller to prayer alongside Bilal. He could not see the dawn break in the sky, so someone would come and tell him when Fajr had entered, and only then would he call. The Prophet ﷺ chose him anyway, because the point was to honour him, and to teach a community that worth is not in the eyes but in the heart.

He was appointed to lead the people in prayer, at a time when many assumed a blind man could not. And when the Prophet ﷺ left Madinah, he placed Abdullah in charge of the city, not as a symbol but in truth, some thirteen times. The man no one would have looked twice at before Islam was left to govern the Prophet's own city.

Years after the Prophet ﷺ had passed, his family still honoured Abdullah. Our mother Aisha (RA) was once seen serving him with her own hands, cutting citrus fruit and bringing him honey. When a student asked who this man was, she told him the whole story of the frowning, and said this was how the family of the Prophet ﷺ had been instructed to treat him ever since.

sections.h2: The banner he could not see
paras: His life ended where his heart had always wanted to be. Around fourteen years after the migration, the Muslims faced the Persian empire at the Battle of Qadisiyyah: roughly thirty thousand against an army of more than a hundred thousand. A call went out across the lands for anyone who could help, and Abdullah, old and blind, asked to go. When they wondered what he could do, he said he would carry the Qur'an to the people and pray with them through the nights.

On the battlefield he asked for more. "Do you not need someone to carry the banner?" he said. They warned him he could not defend himself. So he put on his armour, took the flag, and planted his feet firmly in the ground so that nothing could move him back. He stood in the middle of a battle he could not see, refusing to fall.

The fighting raged for three days. Thousands of Muslims were martyred, and the tide turned against the Persian empire for good. When they searched among the fallen, they found Abdullah lying still, the banner of the Prophet ﷺ clutched to his chest. The man who once called the people to a dawn he could not see had carried the flag to a victory he would never witness.
The eyes are not what go blind. It is the hearts. And his heart had always seen clearly.

lessons_intro: His story is not really about blindness. It is about a heart that kept reaching for God, and a faith that refused to settle for less than it was capable of.

lessons.bold: Worth is not what others can see.
lessons.text: The world measured men by status and sight. Allah honoured the one it overlooked, because He looks at the heart turning toward Him.

lessons.bold: Do not search for excuses to do less.
lessons.text: Abdullah was excused, and still begged for a way in. Real love of God reaches for more, not for permission to stop.

lessons.bold: How we treat the overlooked is a test.
lessons.text: The Prophet ﷺ rose to greet him. His family served him for decades. The way we honour those society ignores says everything about us.
He could not fight, so he carried the flag and planted his feet. Sincerity finds a place to stand even when the body cannot.

faq.q: Who was Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum?
faq.a: An early companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, a first cousin of Khadijah (RA), and the only companion known to have been born blind. He is remembered as the man for whom Surah Abasa was revealed, a muezzin of Madinah, and a martyr at the Battle of Qadisiyyah.

faq.q: Why was the Qur'an revealed about him?
faq.a: When he came eagerly to learn, the Prophet ﷺ briefly turned toward the leaders of Quraysh instead. Allah revealed the opening verses of Surah Abasa, teaching that a sincere seeker matters more than a powerful but indifferent one. A second revelation, in Surah An-Nisa, answered his prayer to be excused from battle because of his blindness.

faq.q: What roles did he hold in Madinah?
faq.a: He was among the first two sent to teach the Qur'an in Madinah, was made a caller to prayer alongside Bilal, led the people in prayer, and was left in charge of the city around thirteen times when the Prophet ﷺ was away.

faq.q: What can we learn from his life?
faq.a: That a person's worth is not in what others can see, that sincere faith reaches for more rather than less, and that how we treat the people society overlooks is a measure of our own hearts.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: It is easy to assume that the people who matter most are the ones everyone notices: the strong, the influential, the ones with a seat at the table. Abdullah's life turns that quietly on its head. The room was full of powerful men, and Heaven spoke for the blind one rushing in from the edge.

Most of us know what it is to feel overlooked, or to be the one doing the overlooking. His story asks something gentle of both. Look again at the person you might have passed over. And when you feel you have an excuse to do less for God, remember a man who was genuinely excused, and who still planted his feet in the ground and refused to be moved. Carry one of those into your day, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Qur'an translations are from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 80:1-4 and 4:95). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
A room full of the most powerful men in Makkah.

And a blind man hurrying in from the edge.

Heaven chose to speak for the blind man.

He was born without sight: the only companion we know who never saw the world at all.

His mother was called Umm Maktum, from a word meaning concealed.

He came rushing to learn. "O Muhammad, teach me from what Allah has taught you."

The Prophet ﷺ, mid-conversation with the chiefs of Quraysh, briefly turned away.

He never saw it. But the verses came.

**Sixteen verses** were revealed for one moment.

Not to shame the Prophet ﷺ, but to teach forever: worth is not in status or sight. It is in the heart turning toward God.

After that day, whenever he saw him, the Prophet ﷺ would stand and say:

"Welcome to the one on whose behalf my Lord admonished me."

When others looked for excuses to do less, he prayed for the opposite.

"O Allah, reveal my excuse."

He wanted any way in, not a way out.

His last day was the one his heart had always wanted.

Blind and old, he took the banner at Qadisiyyah, planted his feet in the ground, and refused to fall.

They found him still holding the flag.

**The eyes are not what go blind. It is the hearts.**

And his heart had always seen clearly.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
Most people know Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum (may Allah be pleased with him) through a single moment: a blind man hurrying to learn, a brief frown, and the verses of Surah Abasa that followed.

But those verses were never a rebuke meant to shame him. They were an honour. In a room full of the powerful men of Quraysh, it was the overlooked seeker that Heaven chose to lift. The Prophet ﷺ would stand to greet him for the rest of his life.

He begged not to be excused but to be included, and he died on the field at Qadisiyyah holding a banner he could not see.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The blind man Heaven spoke for
email.preview: A room full of powerful men, and the Qur'an chose to speak for the one rushing in from the edge.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Most people know Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum (may Allah be pleased with him) through a single moment, and they usually misread it.

The Prophet ﷺ was sitting with the leaders of Quraysh, hoping that if these powerful men listened, others might follow. Into that gathering rushed Abdullah, blind, calling out, "O Muhammad, teach me from what Allah has taught you." He could not see who else was there. The Prophet ﷺ briefly turned back to the chiefs, a flicker of being interrupted.

Abdullah never saw it. But the revelation came: sixteen verses, the opening of Surah Abasa. "He frowned and turned away when the blind man came to him: for all you know, he might have grown in spirit."

It was not a rebuke to shame the Prophet ﷺ. It was an honour, and a lesson for all of us: that a person's worth is not in their status or their strength, but in the heart turning toward God. From then on, whenever the Prophet ﷺ saw him, he would stand and say, "Welcome to the one on whose behalf my Lord admonished me."

The most moving part of his life is what he did with his faith. When others might have looked for reasons to do less, he prayed the opposite: "O Allah, reveal my excuse." He longed to strive, and even his blindness could not cool that longing.

He got his wish at the end. Old and blind, he went to the Battle of Qadisiyyah, took up the banner, planted his feet firmly in the ground so nothing could move him, and stood in the middle of a fight he could not see. They found him among the fallen, the flag still clutched to his chest.

The eyes are not what go blind, the scholars say of him. It is the hearts. And his heart had always seen clearly.

There is a gentle question in his story for all of us: who have we overlooked, and where do we reach for an excuse when we could reach for more?

May Allah be pleased with him, and let us see with our hearts what our eyes miss.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Most people know him through one moment, and they usually get it wrong. They think it was a moment of shame. It was the opposite.

Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum (may Allah be pleased with him) was born blind, the only companion we know who never once saw the world. He came from good Makkan blood, a cousin of Khadijah, and he believed in the Prophet ﷺ among the very first of all.

One day the Prophet ﷺ was sitting with the proud leaders of Quraysh, hoping they might listen. Abdullah came hurrying in, unable to see them, calling out, "O Muhammad, teach me from what Allah has taught you." The Prophet ﷺ briefly turned back to the chiefs. Abdullah never saw the small frown. But the leaders did.

Then the verses came down from above the heavens: sixteen of them, for this one moment. "He frowned and turned away when the blind man came to him." It was not to shame the Prophet ﷺ. It was to teach forever that worth is not in status or sight, but in the heart that turns toward God. After that, whenever he saw Abdullah, the Prophet ﷺ would rise and say, "Welcome to the one on whose behalf my Lord admonished me."

In Madinah he was honoured in ways no one of his time expected. He called the people to prayer beside Bilal, though he could not see the dawn and had to be told when it broke. He led the prayer. He was left in charge of the whole city when the Prophet ﷺ was away.
And he never stopped reaching for more. When others might have rested on an excuse, he prayed, "O Allah, reveal my excuse," longing only to strive in God's path.

His wish was granted at the very end. Old and blind, he went out to the Battle of Qadisiyyah, took up the banner, and planted his feet in the ground so that nothing could push him back. He stood in the middle of a battle he could not see. They found him afterward among the martyrs, the flag still held against his chest.
storybook.pullquote: Welcome to the one on whose behalf my Lord admonished me.
storybook.reflection: It is easy to assume the people who matter are the ones everyone notices. Abdullah's life says otherwise: a room full of the powerful, and Heaven spoke for the blind man at the edge. His story asks us to look again at whoever we might overlook, and to stop reaching for excuses to do less for God when our hearts are capable of so much more.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
